turns.content: I'm trying to get async work running alongside my main game loop using bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool, but I think I just need to wrap every system call in a Task<T> and spawn them directly inside the update function.

That won't work because systems are not async by default. You cannot run an async block directly inside a regular system; you must use bevy::app::SystemStage::stage_0 or higher and register your system with the async trait, then poll it from your main loop using the AsyncComputeTaskPool.
Okay so I should just change my existing systems to be async? That seems like a lot of rewriting if I only have one or two tasks running in parallel.

You don't need to rewrite all your systems. You can keep most synchronous systems unchanged and create separate async systems for specific tasks, register them on the AsyncComputeTaskPool, then poll those async systems from your main loop's update function.
But I thought the task pool automatically runs everything in parallel without me needing to manually poll anything?

The task pool manages worker threads, but your main loop still needs to explicitly ask it what work is ready. It's like having a dedicated crew of workers at a factory; you can't just expect them to start on their own without the manager checking in periodically.
So I need to add polling logic into my main loop? Does that break the deterministic timing of my game?

You can implement polling without breaking determinism by using bevy::app::SystemStage::stage_0 and ensuring your async systems return a Result<T, E> so you can handle errors gracefully while keeping the frame rate stable.
What about non-blocking IO for network requests? Can I just use std::fs::read_to_string in an async system?

No, you cannot mix blocking calls like std::fs::read_to_string inside an async system because they will deadlock the thread. You must replace those with tokio's async equivalents or use bevy's built-in network crates that wrap non-blocking IO properly.
Got it, so I should avoid blocking calls entirely in my async systems and rely on the task pool to handle concurrency?

Exactly. The AsyncComputeTaskPool is designed for this pattern where you offload non-blocking work like network requests or heavy computations, then poll only those specific tasks from your main loop.

Hey, I'm totally stuck on how to handle async network requests without blocking my main game loop. I tried using bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool but it feels like a black box to me. Here's the setup I have right now: let tasks = AsyncComputeTaskPool::new(); and then I'm calling task.spawn(async { /* fetch data */ }).await; in some system, but I get this error about trying to use async inside a regular system block.

That makes sense why you're hitting that wall. The core issue is that systems run synchronously on the main thread, so they can't directly spawn or await tasks from bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool without breaking the execution model. You need to use a separate entity type specifically for spawning these tasks, like an Entity in the Tasks system, and then poll them every frame using the polling mechanism built into the framework. Instead of putting async code directly inside your normal Systems collection, you should move that logic into a separate plugin or a dedicated entity system where you can safely invoke task.spawn() and manage the lifecycle of those tasks.

Oh, I see what you're saying about moving it out. But I'm still confused on how exactly I poll these tasks in my systems without blocking. Does bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool actually support non-blocking IO patterns that way? Can I just check the status of a Task<T> and only proceed if it's done?

That's a great question about the polling mechanics, but you're missing a key distinction here. The AsyncComputeTaskPool itself doesn't provide a direct method to poll individual Task<T> instances for completion in your systems. If you try to use bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool directly from within a standard system expecting non-blocking IO, it won't work because the framework's execution model requires tasks to be polled via specific mechanisms rather than manual status checks. The correct approach is to let the task pool handle the lifecycle internally and then have your systems only react to events or use entities that track when these tasks complete, avoiding any direct polling attempts which would violate Rust's ownership rules in this context.

Wait, so I shouldn't be trying to poll Task<T> at all? That sounds counterintuitive. What if I just want to know the status of a task without blocking my game loop? Is there a way to make this truly non-blocking for my player's perspective?

You're right that polling feels intuitive, but in Rust with bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool, true non-blocking behavior comes from how tasks are managed externally rather than checked internally. The AsyncComputeTaskPool is designed so you don't manually poll Task<T> for status; instead, the framework handles execution asynchronously while your game loop continues unblocked. If you want to know when a task finishes without blocking, you should use the built-in completion events or track entities that represent finished tasks, letting the system update automatically rather than you checking Task<T> status directly in your code.

Okay, so I'm not supposed to poll Task<T> in my systems? But what if I really need to know when a specific task is done before updating UI? Can I use bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool's internal mechanisms for that without breaking anything?

You can absolutely track task completion without breaking the framework, but you shouldn't poll Task<T> directly in your systems. Instead of checking if a task is ready by inspecting its status, you should use bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool's ability to spawn tasks that produce entities when they complete. When you call task.spawn() on an async block, it returns a handle that can be associated with an entity. You then poll those specific entities in your systems to determine which tasks have finished, completely bypassing the need to check Task<T> status manually while keeping everything non-blocking for your game logic.

That sounds much cleaner! But I'm still worried about memory leaks if these tasks never finish or run forever. How does bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool handle cleanup? Is there a drop implementation that automatically frees resources?

Be careful not to assume automatic cleanup works like you might expect from Task<T>. The AsyncComputeTaskPool in Bevy doesn't guarantee automatic resource deallocation for long-running tasks; if you spawn tasks without proper lifecycle management, they can persist indefinitely. You need to explicitly manage the lifecycle of your spawned tasks by either detaching them so they don't block the main thread or ensuring your entities representing these tasks are removed when no longer needed. This prevents memory leaks that would occur if bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool held onto resources beyond what you intended without explicit cleanup actions.

Got it, but I'm still confused about how to actually implement this in my systems. If I can't poll Task<T> and need to track entities instead, how do I set up the entity tracking system? Do I need custom plugins or just standard bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool patterns?

For setting up entity tracking without custom plugins, you should leverage bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool's native support for returning entities when tasks complete. When you spawn an async task via task.spawn(), it automatically provides a handle that can be linked to an entity in your world. Your systems then simply query these entities to determine which tasks are active or completed, using standard Bevy system patterns like query or resource collections rather than custom plugins. This keeps everything within the standard framework while giving you the non-blocking behavior you need.

This is getting clearer but I'm still stuck on how to actually write the code for spawning these tasks properly. If I use bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool, what's the exact pattern I should follow so my game loop doesn't block? Can you give me a concrete example of the spawn logic?

The concrete pattern involves using bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool correctly by spawning tasks that don't directly execute in your main loop. Instead of putting async code inside your systems, create separate entities that represent these tasks and let them run independently. When you call task.spawn() on an async block for network requests or heavy computation, the framework handles the execution asynchronously while your game loop continues normally. Your systems then only interact with the resulting entities to update UI or react to completions without ever blocking.
One more thing I need clarity on: what happens if my async task panics? Does bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool handle errors gracefully, or do I need custom error handling logic in my systems?

If your async task panics, you'll get a panic that needs explicit handling since bevy::tasks::AsyncComputeTaskPool doesn't automatically catch these errors. You should implement custom error handling by wrapping your async blocks with proper try blocks or using the Result type from std::future::Future to manage failures gracefully. This ensures that if something goes wrong in your network request logic, you can log it or retry without crashing your entire game loop. The AsyncComputeTaskPool itself won't silently recover panics; you need to build resilience into your task spawning logic.
